sentences.base_language: It was Kenta's thirty-fifth birthday and his friends had been secretly planning a surprise party for weeks.
sentences.target_language: その日は健太の三十五歳の誕生日で、友人たちは何週間もひそかにサプライズパーティーを計画していた。
sentences.romanized: Sono hi wa Kenta no sanjūgo-sai no tanjōbi de, yūjin-tachi wa nanshūkan mo hisoka ni sapuraizu pātī wo keikaku shite ita.
literal: サプライズパーティーを
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜ていた past progressive**: 計画し**ていた** (had been planning — an ongoing process up to that point). **〜ていた** places an action in continuous progress before the narrative moment: 準備し**ていた** (had been preparing), 練習し**ていた** (had been practising). It establishes that the friends' planning was not a sudden act but a sustained, secret effort.
**〜も emphasis with quantity**: 何週間**も** (for many weeks — as many as that). **〜も** after a quantity adds emphasis, implying the amount is notable: 何時間**も** (for hours and hours), 何度**も** (over and over). **ひそかに** = secretly, quietly, without anyone knowing — an adverb from **ひそか** (na-adjective, secret/quiet): **ひそかに** 思う (harbour a secret thought), **ひそかに** 進める (advance without disclosure).

sentences.base_language: He spent the morning convinced it would be a quiet day — he had told everyone he didn't want any fuss.
sentences.target_language: 健太は、静かな一日になるだろうと思いながら午前中を過ごしていた――誰にも大げさなことはしないでほしいと伝えていたのだ。
sentences.romanized: Kenta wa, shizuka na ichinichi ni naru darō to omoi nagara gozenchū wo sugoshite ita — dare ni mo ōgesa na koto wa shinaide hoshii to tsutaete ita no da.
literal: 静かな一日になるだろうと
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜ながら simultaneous**: 思い**ながら** 過ごしていた (spent the time while thinking). **〜ながら** attaches to the masu-stem and marks two concurrent activities by the same subject — here the background thought colours the whole morning. **〜だろう** conjectural: 静かな一日になる**だろう** (it would probably be a quiet day) — **〜だろう** expresses the speaker's supposition or expectation.
**〜ないでほしい** (want someone not to do ~): しない**でほしい** (want them not to do). **〜てほしい** expresses a desire for another's action: 来**てほしい** (want them to come). Negated as **〜ないでほしい** it conveys a request for restraint. **のだ** explanatory: **伝えていたのだ** explains the preceding statement — 'because he had told everyone beforehand, that is why'.

sentences.base_language: His closest friend called in the afternoon and casually suggested coming over for a simple dinner together.
sentences.target_language: 午後になると、一番親しい友人から電話があって、一緒に簡単な夕飯でもどうかと何気なく誘われた。
sentences.romanized: Gogo ni naru to, ichiban shitashii yūjin kara denwa ga atte, issho ni kantan na yūhan demo dō ka to nani-ge naku sasowareta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜になると** natural-result conditional: 午後**になると** (when afternoon came — as a natural event). **〜と** after a change of state marks what reliably or naturally follows: 春**になると** 花が咲く (when spring comes, flowers bloom). **〜でも** soft suggestion: 夕飯**でも** (dinner or something — a casual, non-demanding proposal). **〜でも** softens an offer: コーヒー**でも** 飲む？ (how about some coffee?), 散歩**でも** しない？ (want to take a walk or something?).
**Passive 〜られる**: 誘わ**れた** (was invited — passive). The passive here shifts focus to Kenta as the recipient of the invitation, without specifying the friend as agent. **何気なく** = casually, without letting anything show — from **何気ない** (nani-ge-nai, nonchalant, offhand): **何気なく** 話す (speak in an offhand way). It signals that the friend deliberately masked her true intentions.

sentences.base_language: When Kenta arrived and the door opened, a room full of people shouted "Surprise!" all at once.
sentences.target_language: 健太が着いてドアが開いたとたん、部屋いっぱいの人たちが一斉に「サプライズ！」と叫んだ。
sentences.romanized: Kenta ga tsuite doa ga hiraita totan, heya ippai no hitotachi ga issei ni 「sapuraizu!」 to sakenda.
literal: 部屋いっぱいの人たちが
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜たとたん** (the moment ~, just as ~): 開い**たとたん** (the moment it opened). **〜たとたん** attaches to the past plain form and marks an immediate, dramatic consequence: 見**たとたん** に笑い出した (burst out laughing the instant I saw it), ドアが開い**たとたん** 冷気が入ってきた (the instant the door opened, cold air came in). It adds immediacy and surprise — fitting here.
**〜いっぱい** (full of ~): 部屋**いっぱい**の人たち (a room full of people). **〜いっぱい** follows a location noun to mean 'packed with' or 'throughout': 教室**いっぱい**の子供たち (a classroom full of children). **一斉に** (issei ni) = all at once, simultaneously, in unison — a common adverb for group actions: **一斉に** 立ち上がった (stood up all at once). The 「」brackets mark direct speech in Japanese text.

sentences.base_language: His heart leaped and he felt tears come to his eyes — he had had absolutely no idea.
sentences.target_language: 胸が躍り、目に涙がにじんできた――まったく気づいていなかったのだ。
sentences.romanized: Mune ga odori, me ni namida ga nijinde kita — mattaku kizuite inakatta no da.
literal: 気づいていなかったのだ。
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜てくる directional**: にじんで**きた** (came welling up — tears moved toward him). **〜てくる** marks change or movement directed toward the speaker's frame: 聞こえて**くる** (sounds coming toward one), 見えて**くる** (comes into view). Here **にじんでくる** vividly conveys the spontaneous arrival of tears in his eyes.
**〜ていなかった past negative progressive**: 気づい**ていなかった** (had not been aware — had had no idea at all). **〜ていなかった** is the negative past of **〜ている**: the process of 'being aware' was entirely absent up to that moment. **のだ** explanatory: **なかったのだ** explains and confirms the reason for his emotional reaction — he truly had no inkling. **まったく** before a negative = absolutely not, not in the least: まったく知らない (don't know at all).

sentences.base_language: The living room was decorated with balloons and colourful lights, and his favourite music was already playing.
sentences.target_language: リビングはバルーンとカラフルなライトで飾られていて、好きな音楽がすでに流れていた。
sentences.romanized: Ribingu wa barūn to karafuru na raito de kazarare ite, suki na ongaku ga sude ni nagare te ita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Passive 〜られていて** chained with te-form: 飾ら**れていて** (was decorated and …). The passive **〜られる** + **〜ている** describes a resultant state brought about by a prior action: 壁に絵が飾ら**れている** (pictures are hung on the wall). The te-form **〜ていて** then connects to the next clause, chaining two background descriptions of the room.
**〜ていた past progressive (background state)**: 流れ**ていた** (was playing/flowing — already in progress). **流れる** (nagareru) = to flow, to play (of music/water) — an evocative verb implying music filling the air rather than merely sounding. **〜ていた** sets the scene: the music was already underway before Kenta's arrival, part of the prepared atmosphere. **すでに** = already — reinforces that the preparations were complete.

sentences.base_language: Friends from every stage of his life were there — old school friends, work colleagues, relatives he hadn't seen in years.
sentences.target_language: 人生のあらゆる時期の友人たちがいた――昔の学校の友人、職場の同僚、何年も会っていなかった親戚まで。
sentences.romanized: Jinsei no arayuru jiki no yūjin-tachi ga ita — mukashi no gakkō no yūjin, shokuba no dōryō, nannen mo atte inakatta shinseki made.
literal: 人生のあらゆる時期の
何年も会っていなかった
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜ていなかった past negative progressive**: 何年も会っ**ていなかった** 親戚 (relatives he hadn't seen for years). **〜ていなかった** is the negative past progressive: the state of 'being in contact' had not existed for many years. As a prenominal modifier it describes the relatives directly: 話し**ていなかった** 人 (a person he hadn't been talking to). **何年も** emphasises the long duration.
**〜まで** (even, as far as): 親戚**まで** (even relatives). **〜まで** extends a list to its furthest, most surprising member: 先生**まで** 来てくれた (even the teacher came). Here it builds to a climax — not just close friends, but distant relatives too. **あらゆる** = every, all manner of — a prenominal adjective stronger than **すべての**: **あらゆる** 手段 (every possible means), **あらゆる** 人 (people of all kinds).

sentences.base_language: Kenta was overwhelmed with gratitude and went around the room thanking everyone with a bow and a warm smile.
sentences.target_language: 健太は感謝の気持ちで胸がいっぱいになり、お辞儀をしながら温かい笑顔で一人ひとりにお礼を言って回った。
sentences.romanized: Kenta wa kansha no kimochi de mune ga ippai ni nari, ojigi wo shi nagara atatakaii egao de hitori-hitori ni orei wo itte mawatta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜ようになる** / **〜いっぱいになる** change-of-state: 胸がいっぱい**になり** (his heart became full — reached a new state). **〜になる** marks a transition into a state: 嬉しく**なる** (become happy), いっぱい**になる** (become full). The conjunctive **〜なり** (= **〜なって**) links it to the next clause, indicating the state led to the subsequent action.
**〜ながら simultaneous**: お辞儀をし**ながら** 言って回った (went around thanking people while bowing). **〜ながら** here shows two simultaneous physical acts — bowing and thanking happen together, painting a vivid picture of gracious gratitude. **〜て回る** (go around doing ~): 言っ**て回る** (go around saying / make the rounds saying). **〜て回る** combines a verb with **回る** (to go around) to express doing something repeatedly to multiple people or places: 挨拶し**て回る** (go around greeting people).

sentences.base_language: The table was covered with his favourite foods, including a home-style simmered dish his mother used to make.
sentences.target_language: テーブルには好きな食べ物がいっぱい並んでいて、お母さんがよく作ってくれた家庭的な煮物も用意されていた。
sentences.romanized: Tēburu ni wa suki na tabemono ga ippai narande ite, okāsan ga yoku tsukutte kureta kateiteki na nimono mo yōi sarete ita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜てくれた habitual benefactive past**: よく作っ**てくれた** (used to make for him — with affection). **よく** + past form marks habitual past action: よく食べ**た** (used to eat often). Combined with **〜てくれた**, it encodes warmth and nostalgia — his mother used to make this dish as a kindness for him. The use of **お母さん** (mother, with honorific **お**) is affectionate rather than formal.
**Passive resultant state 〜されていた**: 用意さ**れていた** (had been prepared — was in a state of readiness). **〜されていた** is the passive of **用意する** combined with **〜ていた**, describing a state achieved through prior preparation: 席が用意さ**れていた** (the seat had been prepared). **煮物** (nimono) = simmered dish — a defining genre of Japanese home cooking where vegetables, tofu, or fish are simmered in a seasoned dashi-based broth: **肉じゃが**, **筑前煮**, etc.

sentences.base_language: Someone poured glasses of sparkling wine and everyone raised a toast to Kenta's health and happiness.
sentences.target_language: 誰かがスパークリングワインをグラスに注いでくれて、みんな健太の健康と幸せを願って乾杯した。
sentences.romanized: Dareka ga supākulingu wain wo gurasu ni soide kurete, minna Kenta no kenkō to shiawase wo negatte kanpai shita.
literal: スパークリングワインを
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜てくれる benefactive**: 注いで**くれて** (poured for everyone — doing it as a kind act for the group). **〜てくれる** marks that the action benefits the speaker's in-group: 手伝っ**てくれた** (helped us), 持っ**てくれた** (carried it for us). Chained with **〜て**, it leads into the next action — the pouring enabled the toast.
**〜て** causal chain: 願っ**て** 乾杯した (toasted, wishing for …). The te-form here expresses the emotional basis for the action that follows: wishing for Kenta's happiness is the sentiment that animates the toast. **乾杯する** (kanpai suru) = to toast, to clink glasses — a fixed social expression: **乾杯！** (Cheers!). **願う** (negau) = to wish for, to hope for — **健康と幸せを願う** (wish for health and happiness) is a set phrase used in toasts and well-wishes.

sentences.base_language: Kenta gave a short, heartfelt speech and tried to express how much everyone in the room meant to him.
sentences.target_language: 健太は短いながらも心のこもったスピーチをして、部屋にいる全員が自分にとってどれほど大切かを伝えようとした。
sentences.romanized: Kenta wa mijikai nagara mo kokoro no komotta supīchi wo shite, heya ni iru zen'in ga jibun ni totte dore hodo taisetsu ka wo tsutaeyō to shita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜ながらも concessive**: 短い**ながらも** (short though it was / even though brief). **〜ながらも** attaches to a plain form and concedes a quality while asserting something that coexists with or despite it: 小さい**ながらも** 立派な家 (a fine house, small though it is). It is more literary than **〜けれど** and adds an appreciative tone.
**〜ようとする** (try to do ~): 伝えよう**とした** (tried to convey). **〜ようとする** = **volitional form** + **とする** — making an attempt at an action, particularly one that requires effort: 言おう**とした** (tried to say), 立とう**とした** (tried to stand). **どれほど〜か** indirect question: **どれほど大切か** (how important they are) embedded as a noun clause object of **伝える**. **〜にとって** = from the standpoint of, for: 私**にとって** (for me, as far as I am concerned).

sentences.base_language: As the evening went on, someone turned the music louder and the living room became an impromptu dance floor.
sentences.target_language: 夜が深まるにつれて、誰かが音楽を大きくすると、リビングはいつの間にか即興のダンスフロアになっていった。
sentences.romanized: Yoru ga fukamaru ni tsurete, dareka ga ongaku wo ōkiku suru to, ribingu wa itsuno mani ka sokkyō no dansu furoa ni natte itta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜につれて** gradual progression: 深まる**につれて** (as the evening deepened — gradually, in step with). **〜につれて** expresses two things changing in tandem: 時間が経つ**につれて** (as time passes), 成長する**につれて** (as one grows). It implies a smooth, natural co-progression rather than a sudden shift.
**〜ていく directional**: なっ**ていった** (went on becoming — a change moving away from the present moment, continuing into the future). **〜ていく** marks change or action that progresses outward/forward in time: 変わっ**ていく** (keep changing), 広まっ**ていく** (keep spreading). **いつの間にか** (itsunomanika) = before one knows it, unnoticed — a fixed adverb: **いつの間にか** 暗くなっていた (it had gotten dark before anyone noticed). **即興の** (sokkyō no) = impromptu, off-the-cuff — from **即興** (improvisation).

sentences.base_language: His feet and back began to ache from dancing but he couldn't bring himself to stop.
sentences.target_language: 踊りすぎて足と腰が痛くなってきたが、健太はやめずにはいられなかった。
sentences.romanized: Odori sugite ashi to koshi ga itaku natte kita ga, Kenta wa yamezu ni wa irarenakatta.
literal: やめずにはいられなかった。
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜ずにはいられない** (cannot help doing ~, cannot bring oneself not to ~): やめ**ずにはいられなかった** (couldn't bring himself to stop — literally 'could not be without not-stopping'). This double-negative construction expresses an irresistible compulsion: 笑わ**ずにはいられない** (can't help laughing), 言わ**ずにはいられない** (have to say it). The past negative **いられなかった** means the compulsion was felt in the past.
**〜すぎる** (do too much): 踊り**すぎて** (having danced too much — te-form). **〜すぎる** attaches to the masu-stem to express excess: 食べ**すぎる** (eat too much), 働き**すぎる** (overwork). The te-form **〜すぎて** introduces the result or consequence: 食べ**すぎて** 気分が悪い (feel sick from eating too much). **〜てくる** directional: 痛くなっ**てきた** (began to ache — the ache came, arriving at him). **〜てくる** marks the onset of a sensation or change approaching the present.

sentences.base_language: When the birthday cake appeared — a beautiful three-tiered cake blazing with candles — everyone began to sing.
sentences.target_language: ろうそくがともるたくさんの火が輝く美しい三段のバースデーケーキが現れたとたん、みんなが歌い始めた。
sentences.romanized: Rōsoku ga tomoru takusan no hi ga kagayaku utsukushii sandan no bāsudē kēki ga arawareta totan, minna ga utai hajimeta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜たとたん** (the moment ~): 現れ**たとたん** (the moment it appeared). **〜たとたん** attaches to the past plain form and marks an immediate dramatic consequence. It conveys the spontaneous, group reaction triggered by the cake's appearance — no deliberation, just an instant communal response.
**〜始める** (begin to ~): 歌い**始めた** (began to sing). **〜始める** attaches to the masu-stem and marks the start of an action: 走り**始める** (begin to run), 泣き**始める** (start crying). It focuses on the moment of onset. Stacked prenominal modifiers before **バースデーケーキ** illustrate Japanese's ability to build complex noun phrases: ろうそくがともる / たくさんの火が輝く / 美しい三段の — each clause modifying the same head noun.

sentences.base_language: Kenta closed his eyes, made a silent wish, and blew out all the candles in one breath.
sentences.target_language: 健太は目を閉じて、心の中でそっと願い事をしてから、一息でろうそくを全部吹き消した。
sentences.romanized: Kenta wa me wo tojite, kokoro no naka de sotto negaigoto wo shite kara, hito-iki de rōsoku wo zenbu fukikeshita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜てから sequence**: して**から** (after making a wish — and then). **〜てから** marks a clear sequential order where the first action is fully completed before the second begins: 食べ**てから** 出かける (go out after eating). It is more explicit about completion than a simple te-form chain, emphasising that the wish came first.
**〜ておく preparation (implicit)**: 願い事をし**ておく** — the wish is made as a preparatory step before blowing. Though **〜ておく** is not overtly present, the **〜てから** construction encodes the same sequential logic of doing something in advance. **吹き消す** (fukikesu) = to blow out — a compound verb: **吹く** (fuku, to blow) + **消す** (kesu, to extinguish). **そっと** = softly, gently, quietly — a mimetic adverb: **そっと** 触れる (touch softly), **そっと** 置く (place quietly). **一息で** = in one breath — a fixed expression meaning without interruption.

sentences.base_language: The cake was extraordinary — light and moist, with a cream that tasted like something from his childhood.
sentences.target_language: ケーキは格別だった――ふんわりしっとりとしていて、クリームは子供のころの記憶を呼び起こすような味がした。
sentences.romanized: Kēki wa kakubetsu datta — funwari shittori to shite ite, kurīmu wa kodomo no koro no kioku wo yobiokosu yō na aji ga shita.
literal: ――ふんわりしっとりとしていて、
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜ような** (like ~, of the kind that ~): 呼び起こす**ような** 味 (a taste that evokes / tastes like something that evokes). **〜ような** follows a plain verb or noun phrase and modifies the following noun with a comparison or resemblance: 夢の**ような** 話 (a story like a dream), 知っている**ような** 気がする (feel as if one knows). Here it expresses that the taste was of a quality or character that called up childhood memories.
**Mimetic adverbs**: **ふんわり** (soft, fluffy, light — like a cloud) and **しっとり** (moist, soft, gentle in texture). Mimetic adverbs are a rich feature of Japanese: **ふんわり** した食感 (a fluffy texture), **しっとり** した生地 (moist dough). They convey sensory qualities vividly and cannot easily be translated word-for-word. **呼び起こす** (yobiokosu) = to call up, to evoke — **呼ぶ** (call) + **起こす** (rouse/bring up): **記憶を呼び起こす** (evoke memories) is a common collocation.

sentences.base_language: Long after midnight, the last guests began saying their goodbyes at the door with warm embraces.
sentences.target_language: 日付が変わってずいぶん経ってから、最後の客たちが温かく抱き合いながら玄関でお別れを言い始めた。
sentences.romanized: Hizuke ga kawatte zuibun tatte kara, saigo no kyaku-tachi ga atatakaku dakai nagara genkan de owakare wo ii hajimeta.
literal: 温かく抱き合いながら
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜てから after**: 経っ**てから** (after considerable time had passed). **〜てから** marks sequential completion: **日付が変わってずいぶん経ってから** = long after midnight had passed. Chaining two te-forms before **〜てから** creates a layered timeline: (1) the date changed, (2) much time passed, (3) then the guests began to leave.
**〜合う reciprocal + 〜ながら simultaneous**: 抱き**合い**ながら (while embracing each other). **抱き合う** = to embrace each other — **抱く** (embrace) + **合う** (reciprocal). Combined with **〜ながら**, it shows the farewells and embraces happening simultaneously. **玄関** (genkan) = the entrance hall of a Japanese home — a culturally specific space where shoes are removed and guests are formally received or seen off. **ずいぶん** = considerably, quite a lot — a degree adverb: **ずいぶん** 遅い (quite late).

sentences.base_language: When everyone had finally left, Kenta stood alone in the quiet room, still glowing with warmth.
sentences.target_language: みんなが帰ってしまうと、健太は静かになった部屋にひとり立って、まだ胸の中に温もりが広がっているのを感じていた。
sentences.romanized: Minna ga kaette shimau to, Kenta wa shizuka ni natta heya ni hitori tatte, mada mune no naka ni nukumori ga hirogatte iru no wo kanjite ita.
literal: みんなが帰ってしまうと、
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜てしまう completion with nuance**: 帰っ**てしまうと** (when everyone had finally left — the finality is tinged with bittersweetness). **〜てしまう** marks completion, often with an emotional undertone of finality or regret: 食べ**てしまった** (ate it all up), 終わっ**てしまった** (it's all over). Here the bittersweet sense that the wonderful party was now over.
**〜のを感じる** nominalised perception: 広がっている**のを** 感じていた (was feeling the warmth spreading — felt the fact/sense of it spreading). **〜のを** nominalises a verb phrase and marks it as the object of a perception verb: 動く**のを** 見た (saw it moving), 笑う**のを** 聞いた (heard them laughing). **温もり** (nukumori) = warmth, human warmth — a softer, more emotional word than **暖かさ**: **人の温もり** (the warmth of human connection). **〜ていた** progressive: he was in the process of feeling this warmth.

sentences.base_language: He sent a long voice message to his closest friend, thanking her for organizing everything.
sentences.target_language: 健太は一番親しい友人に長いボイスメッセージを送って、すべてを準備してくれたことへの感謝を伝えた。
sentences.romanized: Kenta wa ichiban shitashii yūjin ni nagai boisu messēji wo okute, subete wo junbi shite kureta koto e no kansha wo tsutaeta.
literal: 長いボイスメッセージを
すべてを準備してくれた
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜てくれた benefactive** nominalised: 準備し**てくれた** こと (the fact that she prepared everything for him). Nominalising a **〜てくれた** phrase with **こと** turns a benefactive action into a noun that can be referenced: 助け**てくれたこと** への感謝 (gratitude for having helped). **〜ことへの** (gratitude directed toward the fact of ~) is a formal, written-register structure.
**〜てもらう** (have someone do for one): an alternative frame would be 準備し**てもらった**こと — in this sentence **〜てくれた** is used, encoding Kenta's appreciation from his own perspective. **〜てくれる** centres the giver's action as a gift; **〜てもらう** centres the receiver's receipt. Both are benefactive. **伝える** (tsutaeru) = to convey, to communicate — often used for emotions and messages: 感謝を**伝える** (express gratitude), 気持ちを**伝える** (convey one's feelings).

sentences.base_language: Lying in bed that night, he thought this was exactly the kind of evening that stays with you for the rest of your life.
sentences.target_language: その夜、布団の中で、こういう夜こそ一生忘れられないものだと健太はしみじみと思った。
sentences.romanized: Sono yoru, futon no naka de, kō iu yoru koso isshō wasurerarenai mono da to Kenta wa shimijimi to omotta.
literal: 忘れられないものだと
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜と思う** reported thought: 忘れられないものだ**と思った** (thought that this was something one cannot forget). **〜と思う** embeds a plain-form statement as reported or internal thought: 正しい**と思う** (I think it's right), 来ない**と思った** (thought they wouldn't come). Here the plain form **ものだ** (it is the kind of thing that …) is embedded before **と**.
**〜こそ emphatic topic marker**: こういう夜**こそ** (exactly this kind of evening — this above all). **〜こそ** replaces **は** or **が** and singles out the marked element as the definitive one: これ**こそ** 本物だ (this, above all, is the real thing), 今**こそ** チャンスだ (now is precisely the chance). **〜ものだ** (it is the kind of thing that ~, one naturally ~): 忘れられない**ものだ** = it is just the kind of thing one cannot forget — **〜ものだ** expresses a general truth or natural expectation. **しみじみと** = with deep, quiet feeling, heartfelt — a mimetic adverb: **しみじみと** 感じる (feel deeply), **しみじみと** 思う (reflect with heartfelt emotion). A perfect closing note.
